display_name: BGP peer going Established->Idle once is a reset, not a flap — check state time
context: show bgp summary output showing a peer in Idle or Active state with a low uptime/state timer
observation: A BGP peer shows state Idle or Active and the up/down timer shows a recent time (minutes or hours)
naive_interpretation: The BGP session is flapping and unstable
correct_interpretation: A single transition from Established to Idle is a session reset, not a flap. Flapping means the session is cycling between Established and down states repeatedly. Check the state change counter and look at the time pattern. A peer that went down once and stayed down has a different root cause (likely a deliberate action or single event) than one cycling every few minutes (likely a hold timer or route-related issue).
why: The up/down field shows time since last state change — a short time could mean one recent event, not ongoing instability
True flapping produces high state change counters and a pattern of short up/down intervals in the logs
A single reset is often caused by a maintenance action, a single link failure, or a one-time configuration change on the remote end

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check the state change counter and last reset reason to distinguish single event from flapping
Look for a pattern of repeated up/down transitions versus a single event
Check state change count and reset reason

traps: Declaring a peer as flapping based on a single down event
Not checking the state change counter to see if this is a one-time or repeated event
Not reading the last reset reason — it often points directly to the cause (hold timer expired, admin reset, notification received)
Conflating the concepts of down and flapping when they have different troubleshooting approaches
